In Western, Northern, and Central Europe (France, Norway, Denmark, the Low Countries, and the [annexed portions of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Bohemia_and_Moravia "Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia")) Germany established economic policies through which it collected roughly 69.5 billion reichsmarks (27.8 billion US dollars) by the end of the war; this figure does not include the [plunder](/wiki/Nazi_plunder "Nazi plunder") of industrial products, military equipment, raw materials and other goods.[[417]](#cite_note-421) Thus, the income from occupied nations was over 40 percent of the income Germany collected from taxation, a figure which increased to nearly 40 percent of total German income as the war went on.[[418]](#cite_note-Milward_1979_138-422)

In the East, the intended gains of *[Lebensraum](/wiki/Lebensraum "Lebensraum")* were never attained as fluctuating front-lines and Soviet [scorched earth](/wiki/Scorched_earth "Scorched earth") policies denied resources to the German invaders.[[419]](#cite_note-Milward_1992_148-423) Unlike in the West, the [Nazi racial policy](/wiki/Racial_policy_of_Nazi_Germany "Racial policy of Nazi Germany") encouraged extreme brutality against what it considered to be the "[inferior people](/wiki/Untermensch "Untermensch")" of [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_peoples "Slavic peoples") descent; most German advances were thus followed by [mass atrocities and war crimes](/wiki/Generalplan_Ost "Generalplan Ost").[[420]](#cite_note-424) The Nazis [killed an estimated 2.8 million ethnic Poles](/wiki/Nazi_crimes_against_the_Polish_nation "Nazi crimes against the Polish nation") in addition to Polish-Jewish [victims of the Holocaust](/wiki/Victims_of_the_Holocaust "Victims of the Holocaust").[[421]](#cite_note-425) Although by 1942 [resistance groups](/wiki/Resistance_during_World_War_II "Resistance during World War II") formed in most occupied territories,[[422]](#cite_note-426) the assessments of the effectiveness of [Soviet partisans](/wiki/Soviet_partisans "Soviet partisans")[[423]](#cite_note-427) and [French Resistance](/wiki/French_Resistance "French Resistance")[[424]](#cite_note-428) suggests that they did not significantly hamper German operations until late 1943.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-031-2436-03A%2C_Russland%2C_Hinrichtung_von_Partisanen_retouched.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-031-2436-03A%2C_Russland%2C_Hinrichtung_von_Partisanen_retouched.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-031-2436-03A,_Russland,_Hinrichtung_von_Partisanen_retouched.jpg)

[Soviet partisans](/wiki/Soviet_partisans "Soviet partisans") hanged by the German army. The [Russian Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Russian_Academy_of_Sciences "Russian Academy of Sciences") reported in 1995 that [civilian victims in the Soviet Union](/wiki/World_War_II_casualties_of_the_Soviet_Union "World War II casualties of the Soviet Union") at German hands totalled 13.7 million dead, twenty percent of the 68 million people in the occupied Soviet Union.

In Asia, Japan termed nations under its occupation as being part of the [Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere](/wiki/Greater_East_Asia_Co-Prosperity_Sphere "Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere"), essentially a Japanese [hegemony](/wiki/Hegemony "Hegemony") which it claimed was for purposes of liberating colonised peoples.[[425]](#cite_note-429) Although Japanese forces were sometimes welcomed as liberators from European domination, [Japanese war crimes](/wiki/Japanese_war_crimes "Japanese war crimes") frequently turned local public opinion against them.[[426]](#cite_note-GSWW6_266-430) During Japan's initial conquest, it captured 4,000,000 barrels (640,000 m3) of oil (~550,000 tonnes) left behind by retreating Allied forces; and by 1943, was able to get production in the Dutch East Indies up to 50 million barrels (7,900,000 m3) of oil (~6.8 million tonnes), 76 percent of its 1940 output rate.[[426]](#cite_note-GSWW6_266-430)

### Home fronts and production

Main articles: [Military production during World War II](/wiki/Military_production_during_World_War_II "Military production during World War II") and [Home front during World War II](/wiki/Home_front_during_World_War_II "Home front during World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/World_War_II_Allies_to_Axis_GDP_Ratio.svg/500px-World_War_II_Allies_to_Axis_GDP_Ratio.svg.png)](/wiki/File:World_War_II_Allies_to_Axis_GDP_Ratio.svg)

Allies to Axis GDP ratio throughout the war

In the 1930s, Britain and the United States together controlled almost 75% of world mineral output—essential for projecting military power.[[427]](#cite_note-431)

In Europe, before the outbreak of the war, the Allies had significant advantages in both population and economics. In 1938, the Western Allies (United Kingdom, France, Poland and the British Dominions) had a 30 percent larger population and a 30 percent higher gross domestic product than the European Axis powers (Germany and Italy); including colonies, the Allies had more than a 5:1 advantage in population and a nearly 2:1 advantage in GDP.[[428]](#cite_note-6Econ3-432) In Asia at the same time, China had roughly six times the population of Japan but only an 89 percent higher GDP; this reduces to three times the population and only a 38 percent higher GDP if Japanese colonies are included.[[428]](#cite_note-6Econ3-432)

The United States produced about two-thirds of all munitions used by the Allies in World War II, including warships, transports, warplanes, artillery, tanks, trucks, and ammunition.[[429]](#cite_note-433) Although the Allies' economic and population advantages were largely mitigated during the initial rapid blitzkrieg attacks of Germany and Japan, they became the decisive factor by 1942, after the United States and Soviet Union joined the Allies and the war evolved into one of [attrition](/wiki/Attrition_warfare "Attrition warfare").[[430]](#cite_note-6Econ2-434) While the Allies' ability to out-produce the Axis was partly due to more access to natural resources, other factors, such as Germany and Japan's reluctance to employ women in the [labour force](/wiki/Workforce "Workforce"),[[431]](#cite_note-435) Allied [strategic bombing](/wiki/Strategic_bombing_during_World_War_II "Strategic bombing during World War II"),[[432]](#cite_note-436) and Germany's late shift to a [war economy](/wiki/War_economy "War economy")[[433]](#cite_note-437) contributed significantly. Additionally, neither Germany nor Japan planned to fight a protracted war, and had not equipped themselves to do so.[[434]](#cite_note-438) Germany, Japan and the Soviet Union used millions of [slave labourers](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") in war-related industries.[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerbert2014-411)[[409]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKratoska2015xv-413)[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies2014-393)

### Advances in technology and its application

Main article: [Technology during World War II](/wiki/Technology_during_World_War_II "Technology during World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1978-Anh.026-01%2C_Peenem%C3%BCnde%2C_V2_beim_Start.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1978-Anh.026-01%2C_Peenem%C3%BCnde%2C_V2_beim_Start.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1978-Anh.026-01,_Peenem%C3%BCnde,_V2_beim_Start.jpg)

A [V-2 rocket](/wiki/V-2_rocket "V-2 rocket") launched from a fixed site in [Peenemünde](/wiki/Peenem%C3%BCnde "Peenemünde"), 21 June 1943

Aircraft were used for [reconnaissance](/wiki/Reconnaissance_aircraft "Reconnaissance aircraft"), as [fighters](/wiki/Fighter_aircraft "Fighter aircraft"), [bombers](/wiki/Bomber "Bomber"), and [ground-support](/wiki/Close_air_support "Close air support"), and each role developed considerably. Innovations included [airlift](/wiki/Airlift "Airlift") (the capability to quickly move limited high-priority supplies, equipment, and personnel);[[435]](#cite_note-EncWWII_76-439) and [strategic bombing](/wiki/Strategic_bombing "Strategic bombing") (the bombing of enemy industrial and population centres to destroy the enemy's ability to wage war).[[436]](#cite_note-440) [Anti-aircraft weaponry](/wiki/Anti-aircraft_warfare "Anti-aircraft warfare") also advanced, including defences such as [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") and surface-to-air artillery, in particular the introduction of the [proximity fuze](/wiki/Proximity_fuze "Proximity fuze"). The use of the [jet aircraft](/wiki/Jet_aircraft "Jet aircraft") was pioneered and led to jets becoming standard in air forces worldwide.[[437]](#cite_note-441)

Advances were made in nearly every aspect of [naval warfare](/wiki/Naval_warfare "Naval warfare"), most notably with [aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") and [submarines](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine"). Although [aeronautical](/wiki/Aeronautics "Aeronautics") warfare had relatively little success at the start of the war, [actions at Taranto](/wiki/Battle_of_Taranto "Battle of Taranto"), [Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor"), and the [Coral Sea](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Coral_Sea "Battle of the Coral Sea") established the carrier as the dominant capital ship (in place of the battleship).[[438]](#cite_note-EncWWII_163-442)[[439]](#cite_note-443)[[440]](#cite_note-444) In the Atlantic, [escort carriers](/wiki/Escort_carrier "Escort carrier") became a vital part of Allied convoys, increasing the effective protection radius and helping to close the [Mid-Atlantic gap](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_gap "Mid-Atlantic gap").[[441]](#cite_note-445) Carriers were also more economical than [battleships](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship") due to the relatively low cost of aircraft[[442]](#cite_note-446) and because they are not required to be as heavily armoured.[[443]](#cite_note-447) Submarines, which had proved to be an effective weapon during the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"),[[444]](#cite_note-Bur&Ryd_1995_15-448) were expected by all combatants to be important in the second. The British focused development on [anti-submarine](/wiki/Anti-submarine_warfare "Anti-submarine warfare") [weaponry](/wiki/Anti-submarine_weapon "Anti-submarine weapon") and tactics, such as [sonar](/wiki/Sonar "Sonar") and convoys, while Germany focused on improving its offensive capability, with designs such as the [Type VII submarine](/wiki/Type_VII_submarine "Type VII submarine") and [wolfpack](/wiki/Wolfpack_(naval_tactic) "Wolfpack (naval tactic)") tactics.[[445]](#cite_note-Bur&Ryd_1995_16-449) Gradually, improving Allied technologies such as the [Leigh Light](/wiki/Leigh_Light "Leigh Light"), [Hedgehog](/wiki/Hedgehog_(weapon) "Hedgehog (weapon)"), [Squid](/wiki/Squid_(weapon) "Squid (weapon)"), and [homing torpedoes](/wiki/Mark_24_mine "Mark 24 mine") proved effective against German submarines.[[446]](#cite_note-450)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Trinity_device_readied.jpg/250px-Trinity_device_readied.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trinity_device_readied.jpg)

Nuclear *Gadget* being raised to the top of the detonation "shot tower", at [Alamogordo Bombing Range](/wiki/Alamogordo_Bombing_and_Gunnery_Range "Alamogordo Bombing and Gunnery Range"); [Trinity nuclear test](/wiki/Trinity_(nuclear_test) "Trinity (nuclear test)"), [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), July 1945

[Land warfare](/wiki/Land_warfare "Land warfare") changed from the static frontlines of [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare") of World War I, which had relied on improved [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") that outmatched the speed of both [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") and [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry"), to increased mobility and [combined arms](/wiki/Combined_arms "Combined arms"). The [tank](/wiki/Tank "Tank"), which had been used predominantly for infantry support in the First World War, had evolved into the primary weapon.[[447]](#cite_note-EncWWII_125-451) In the late 1930s, tank design was considerably more advanced than it had been during World War I,[[448]](#cite_note-452) and [advances continued throughout the war](/wiki/Tanks_in_World_War_II "Tanks in World War II") with increases in speed, armour and firepower.[[449]](#cite_note-453)[[450]](#cite_note-454) At the start of the war, most commanders thought enemy tanks should be met by tanks with superior specifications.[[451]](#cite_note-EncWWII_108-455) This idea was challenged by the poor performance of the relatively light early tank guns against armour, and German doctrine of avoiding tank-versus-tank combat. This, along with Germany's use of combined arms, were among the key elements of their highly successful blitzkrieg tactics across Poland and France.[[447]](#cite_note-EncWWII_125-451) Many means of [destroying tanks](/wiki/Anti-tank_warfare "Anti-tank warfare"), including [indirect artillery](/wiki/Indirect_fire "Indirect fire"), [anti-tank guns](/wiki/Anti-tank_gun "Anti-tank gun") (both towed and [self-propelled](/wiki/Self-propelled_artillery "Self-propelled artillery")), [mines](/wiki/Anti-tank_mine "Anti-tank mine"), short-ranged infantry antitank weapons, and other tanks were used.[[451]](#cite_note-EncWWII_108-455) Even with large-scale mechanisation, infantry remained the backbone of all forces,[[452]](#cite_note-EncWWII_734-456) and throughout the war, most infantry were equipped similarly to World War I.[[453]](#cite_note-Comp_221-457) The portable machine gun spread, a notable example being the German [MG 34](/wiki/MG_34 "MG 34"), and various [submachine guns](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun") which were suited to [close combat](/wiki/Close_combat "Close combat") in urban and jungle settings.[[453]](#cite_note-Comp_221-457) The [assault rifle](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle"), a late war development incorporating many features of the rifle and submachine gun, became the standard post-war infantry weapon for most armed forces.[[454]](#cite_note-458)

Most major belligerents attempted to solve the problems of complexity and security involved in using large [codebooks](/wiki/Codebook "Codebook") for [cryptography](/wiki/Cryptography "Cryptography") by designing [ciphering](/wiki/Cipher "Cipher") machines, the most well-known being the German [Enigma machine](/wiki/Enigma_machine "Enigma machine").[[455]](#cite_note-459) Development of [SIGINT](/wiki/SIGINT "SIGINT") (*sig*nals *int*elligence) and [cryptanalysis](/wiki/Cryptanalysis "Cryptanalysis") enabled the countering process of decryption. Notable examples were the Allied decryption of [Japanese naval codes](/wiki/Japanese_naval_codes "Japanese naval codes")[[456]](#cite_note-Schoenherr-460) and British [Ultra](/wiki/Ultra_(cryptography) "Ultra (cryptography)"), a [pioneering method](/wiki/Bombe#The_British_Bombe "Bombe") for decoding Enigma that benefited from information given to the United Kingdom by the [Polish Cipher Bureau](/wiki/Cipher_Bureau_(Poland)#Gift_to_allies "Cipher Bureau (Poland)"), which had been decoding early versions of Enigma before the war.[[457]](#cite_note-461) Another component of [military intelligence](/wiki/Military_intelligence "Military intelligence") was [deception](/wiki/Deception "Deception"), which the Allies used to great effect in operations such as [Mincemeat](/wiki/Operation_Mincemeat "Operation Mincemeat") and [Bodyguard](/wiki/Operation_Bodyguard "Operation Bodyguard").[[456]](#cite_note-Schoenherr-460)[[458]](#cite_note-462)

Other technological and engineering feats achieved during, or as a result of, the war include the world's first programmable computers ([Z3](/wiki/Z3_(computer) "Z3 (computer)"), [Colossus](/wiki/Colossus_computer "Colossus computer"), and [ENIAC](/wiki/ENIAC "ENIAC")), [guided missiles](/wiki/V-1_flying_bomb "V-1 flying bomb") and [modern rockets](/wiki/V-2_rocket "V-2 rocket"), the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project")'s development of [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon"), [operations research](/wiki/Operations_research "Operations research"), the development of [artificial harbours](/wiki/Mulberry_harbour "Mulberry harbour"), and [oil pipelines under the English Channel](/wiki/Operation_Pluto "Operation Pluto").[[459]](#cite_note-463)[[460]](#cite_note-464) Although [penicillin](/wiki/Penicillin "Penicillin") was discovered before the war, the [development](/wiki/History_of_penicillin "History of penicillin") of industrial production technology as well as the mass production and use began during the war.[[461]](#cite_note-465)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** While [various other dates](#Start_and_end_dates) have been proposed as the date on which World War II began or ended, this is the period most frequently cited.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Often abbreviated as **WWII** or **WW2**
3. **[^](#cite_ref-73)** [The UK declared war on Germany](/wiki/United_Kingdom_declaration_of_war_on_Germany_(1939) "United Kingdom declaration of war on Germany (1939)") at 11 am. [France followed 6 hours later](/wiki/French_declaration_of_war_on_Germany_(1939) "French declaration of war on Germany (1939)") at 5 pm.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-370)** Reparations were exacted from [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Republic "Hungarian People's Republic"), [Romania](/wiki/Romanian_People%27s_Republic "Romanian People's Republic"), and [Bulgaria](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_Bulgaria "People's Republic of Bulgaria") using Soviet-dominated joint enterprises. The Soviet Union also instituted trading arrangements deliberately designed to favour the country. Moscow controlled the Communist parties that ruled the satellite states, and they followed orders from the Kremlin. Historian Mark Kramer concludes: "The net outflow of resources from eastern Europe to the Soviet Union was approximately $15 billion to $20 billion in the first decade after World War II, an amount roughly equal to the total aid provided by the United States to western Europe under the [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan")."

The period featured massacres with victims in the hundreds at [Civitella](/wiki/Civitella_in_Val_di_Chiana "Civitella in Val di Chiana") ([de Grazia & Paggi 1991](#CITEREFde_GraziaPaggi1991); [Belco 2010](#CITEREFBelco2010)), [Fosse Ardeatine](/wiki/Ardeatine_massacre "Ardeatine massacre") ([Portelli 2003](#CITEREFPortelli2003)), and [Sant'Anna di Stazzema](/wiki/Sant%27Anna_di_Stazzema_massacre "Sant'Anna di Stazzema massacre") ([Gordon 2012](#CITEREFGordon2012), pp. 10–11), and is capped with the [Marzabotto massacre](/wiki/Marzabotto_massacre "Marzabotto massacre").
